headline: Nearly Half of Dementia Cases Could Be Prevented, WHO Says
meta_description: WHO's updated guidelines show up to 45% of dementia cases may be preventable through lifestyle changes and disease management.
summary_short: The World Health Organization has released new evidence-based recommendations showing that nearly half of dementia cases could be prevented or delayed.
body:
More than 57 million people worldwide live with dementia today, with nearly 10 million new cases diagnosed every year. But there is reason for hope: the World Health Organization has released updated guidance suggesting that up to 45 percent of cases may be preventable or delayed.

While dementia remains irreversible and without cure, growing scientific evidence shows that modifiable risk factors throughout life can reduce its toll. The WHO recommendations are straightforward: regular physical activity, stopping tobacco use, reducing alcohol consumption, and eating a healthy diet. Managing chronic conditions like high blood pressure, diabetes, and high cholesterol also matters, as does staying cognitively and socially engaged.

The updated guidelines introduce new insights too. For the first time, WHO recommends reducing air pollution exposure as a dementia prevention strategy. Hearing aids may also help lower risk in some people.

Beyond individual health, this matters economically and socially. Dementia costs the global economy around $1.3 trillion annually, with roughly half coming from unpaid family care. By integrating dementia prevention into existing health services, WHO says countries can help more people live longer, healthier, and more independent lives. The knowledge exists—now it's about putting it into practice.
